annotations.comment: great title!

paragraphs.body: When I was a little girl, I was full of joy and carefree energy, completely unaware of how judgmental the world can be. I rocked a mushroom haircut in first grade and even had a short boy cut that barely went past my ears in second. Back then, I didn’t think twice about how I looked or how others might see me as. I didn’t care about being excluded by other kids or feeling different because of my appearance. It was simply something I was never aware of as a kid. I was just me, happy and unbothered. But as I grew older, my carefree attitude was slowly replaced by constant worry, and my hair became the passionate center of my insecurities.

annotations.comment: This is a wonderful intro. Nice :)

paragraphs.body: Every day now, I obsessed over whether my hair was sticking out in odd directions or if it had turned into a frizzy mess after a long day of classes. I can’t explain how much times I would go to the school bathroom just to check on my hair like how a babysitter had to constantly look after a kid. Rainy days were my ultimate nemesis. The humidity seemed to plot against me, turning my hair into a wild, untamed storm of its own. By the time I got home and caught a glimpse of myself in the mirror, I looked like I had just walked off a battlefield. Those moments felt like a passionate defeat, a constant reminder of how much control I lacked over something as simple as my hair. This marked the beginning of how my hair ties became my lifeline necessity.

paragraphs.body: My three hair ties were the only things that could hold back the chaos of my hair. Without them, I felt exposed and incomplete, as if the world would notice every strand out of place. It was after years that I found out that society had so many expectations for women like me, to look polished, effortless, and perfect. If my hair wasn’t pulled into a sleek, tight ponytail, I feared I would be judged, ignored, or even dismissed.

annotations.text: I found out that society had so many expectations for women like
me, to look polished, effortless, and perfect. If my hair wasn’t pulled into a sleek, tight ponytail, I feared I would be judged, ignored, or even dismissed.
annotations.comment: This idea has so much heat and energy! Let's talk about this, explore it, see what we might discover...

paragraphs.body: Three hair bands lie quietly on my desk, their dark loops tangled in a loose, unassembled pile. Each one is a deep, faded black color with a few strands of my wavy, pitch-black hair wrapped around it, stretched just enough to show signs of regular use. As I pick them up, they feel soft and flexible to touch, one being thick and two being thin. Their fabric holds a faint, calming scent of my honey milk shampoo and conditioner, merged with the slightest hint of something metallic. They taste of salt if touched to my lips, and when I stretch it, you can hear the elastic rubber bending inside. They seem almost like a reflection of me: familiar, dependable, and holding onto traces of my insecurities. They’ve been with me ever since I was a child, taming the wavy, uneven strands I’ve always tried to keep in place. The strange and stubborn poofiness after I step out of a hot, steamy shower or the frizz that forms when the cold salty rain pours down from the chilly night sky. Everything seems to calm down with a simple pull-back of these bands. One is not enough to battle the wrath of my hair but three is enough to tame the passionate chaos of my biggest enemy. They make me forget my worries, but just for a moment. Soon these black loops will break as I over stretch them day by day, and soon, it leaves me searching for another. The cycle of starting over as if I’m constantly looking for the next one that’ll be strong enough to manage both my hair and my doubts.

annotations.text: They make me forget my worries, but just for a moment.
annotations.comment: Ah yes. A repetition of ideas! What _are_ your worries? Your insecurities?

annotations.text: They seem almost like a reflection of me: familiar, dependable, and holding onto traces of my insecurities.

paragraphs.body: I realized that without my trusty hair ties, I didn’t feel like I belonged. My hair being untamed and unpredictable felt like a barrier between me and the world that I so bad wanted to fit into. I’ve always wanted the same confidence as all the other popular girls. I couldn’t shake the feeling that appearance mattered more than anything else. If I didn’t look as good as the girl in front of me with her long eyelashes and silky blonde hair, people wouldn’t approach me to be their friend. If I didn’t have the charm of the girl behind me, with her sparkling ear piercings and glossy lips, no one would want me in their group project. And if I didn’t carry the cool nonchalant look or the hourglass figure of the girl sitting next to me, I felt like my chances of making friends would be impossible. It felt unfair but true, popular girls always seemed to get chosen, their appearances acting as golden tickets. This passionate desire to belong pushed me to extremes always striving for perfection thinking it was the only way to be accepted.

annotations.text: I realized that without my trusty hair ties, I didn’t feel like I belonged. My hair being untamed and unpredictable felt like a barrier between me and the world that I so bad wanted to fit into.

paragraphs.body: Years after years of transforming and experimenting with my hair, I have finally come to this day where I feel comfortable with my hair. It wasn’t something I forced myself to do, but it was the fact that it was so exhausting trying to maintain a high expectation of myself just to please others, when in fact I wasn’t even pleasing them it was my idea of thinking I pleased them because I pleased myself.

annotations.text: have finally come to this day where I feel comfortable with my hair. It wasn’t something I forced myself to do, but it was the fact that it was so exhausting trying to maintain a high expectation of myself just to please others, when in fact I wasn’t even pleasing them it was my idea of thinking I pleased them because I pleased myself.
annotations.comment: I'm interested! But let's dig a little deeper, here. Why, specifically, are you now comfortable?

paragraphs.body: My first transformation was back in 6th grade. I would straighten my hair every morning, waking up as early as 5:00 to straighten every strand of my thick, frizzy hair. My hair was so thick and wavy that I only felt comfort if I saw myself in pin-straight hair. It took at least an hour and a half to finish, and right after that, I would tell my grandma to wake up and slick it back to my desire. That was the most stressful part because it wasn’t always that she tied it back the way I wanted, and a lot of the time I would tell her to start over again until I looked in the mirror and had a sense of passionate satisfaction.

annotations.comment: These are fascinating details about all of your hair transformations. I'm interested, but I wonder if they're necessary for the purposes of your essay? How do they support/develop your central focusing idea?

paragraphs.body: Having my hair up felt like a safe place. I never realized how passionately attached I was to the security of my three hair bands until picture day in middle school. It was a regular day for me, but excitement buzzed in the air for everyone else, as it was graduation picture day. My hair had gone through countless phases, with thick, sticky gel to tame it just right. Every morning, I’d wake my grandma as early as 6:00 am because she was the only one who knew how to tie it the exact way I needed it. She’d comb it slowly and carefully, scraping my scalp, shaping it into that perfect tight ponytail. Then, I’d slather gel on top, smoothing it down until it felt stiff and glossy like a shiny Christmas ornament. That morning, I went through the same passionate steps, straightening each strand with care, tying it tightly, and coating it with gel until it held firm. But during choir class, right before my picture appointment, the inevitable happened. Yoyo, with a playful grin, reached over and yanked out my hair ties. My hair shot up, stiff and clumped from the gel, sticking out like a dried-up tree. My scalp burned where the bands had been ripped out, and I felt a cold shiver run down my spine. I froze. My heart pounded, and my face turned red. I could feel each strand held awkwardly by the dried gel, making my head look wild. I was too humiliated to move, and tears blurred my vision as I struggled to process what had just happened. Yoyo’s face filled with concern, not knowing what to do. I texted my grandma, hands shaking, begging her to come fix the disaster. I couldn’t face picture day or anyone looking the way I did. Waiting for my grandma felt like waiting for hell’s door to open. That moment made me realize how deeply I clung to having my hair up, to the fragile sense of control and comfort it gave me. It wasn’t just about the hair or my appearance; it was about holding onto something passionate and familiar in a world that often felt unpredictable.

annotations.text: smoothing it down until it felt stiff and glossy like a shiny Christmas ornament.
annotations.comment: great detail :)

annotations.text: It wasn’t just about the hair or my appearance; it was about holding onto something passionate and familiar in a world that often felt unpredictable.

paragraphs.body: My second transformation came in 7th grade when my mother took me to the salon for a Japanese straight perm. At the time, I thought it was the perfect solution to all my problems, a permanent fix to the chaos that was in my hair. Sitting on that stool, I felt a wave of happiness, convinced that my troubles were finally over. When I left the salon, I felt like the prettiest girl in the room, my hair sleek and perfectly in place for the first time. But as time went on, I realized the damage it had caused. The sad truth is that beauty isn’t permanent, its only temporary. Perfection is just an illusion, I learned it the hard way but the beauty I had been so obsessed with wasn’t something that could be maintained forever, no matter how hard I tried.

annotations.text: The sad truth is that beauty isn’t permanent, its only temporary.
annotations.comment: another interesting idea...but is it a new and different one? Perhaps for a different essay?

paragraphs.body: My third transformation is where I am now, not a slick back or a polished Japanese straight perm, but a simple low ponytail held together by my three trusty hair ties. After years of enduring hairdressers mocking remarks about how weird my natural hair looked and felt and the begging need of reassurance from the public standpoint, I made the decision to stop fixing my hair to please the world. It wasn’t an overnight idea, but a gradual realization that I couldn’t keep erasing parts of myself for societies acceptance. I had a sense of control over myself finally. For me, Control is the sense of belonging and comfort I find in small rituals that help me feel stable and accepted in this unpredictable world. Control doesn’t mean everything will work out the way you want it to. Control is not about having power or having suppression, neither does it mean the pursuit of perfection. Control is my form of mental discipline as opposed to physical force, where I hand on put effort in times when it appears to be a disorderly or disorienting. When I find myself in this situation, it is a routine for me that I find satisfaction doing what pulls me back to reality. At times, control to me is when there are things that I can always count and rely on when nothing seems to go my way. To me, Control is invisible, and it is my job to find myself as I am completely lost, tangled in overwhelming situations. Understanding this has shaped how I see my own story; It's not about the hairstyle or gel, but how these small acts of control give me strength and hope.

annotations.text: Understanding this has shaped how I see my own story;
annotations.comment: I'm also interested in the word. Control can mean so many different things. I'm particularly interested in the way it can mean that you're in control of your own story. Let's talk about how you might leverage this definition paragraph.
